title: Plying a two-ply yarn on a spindle
subtitle: Combining two singles into a balanced, stable yarn
excerpt: Plying twists two singles together in the opposite direction to spinning, balancing the stored twist and producing a round, stable yarn. This tutorial covers the Andean plying bracelet method, which lets a single spinner ply from one full bobbin without a second spindle.
sourceNotes: Plying mechanics and the Andean plying bracelet method are standard handspinning practice. Observation-led.

glossaryTerms.definition: Yarn spun from raw fibre in a single operation. Singles have active twist and will kink back on itself. Plying combines two or more singles to balance the twist and produce a stable yarn.

Twisting two or more singles together in the direction opposite to their original spin. Z-spun singles are plied S-direction. The opposing twists cancel and balance each other, producing a round, stable yarn.
A plied yarn in which the twist in the singles and the plying twist cancel each other out. A balanced yarn hangs in a loop without curling or twisting back on itself.
A method of winding a single from one spindle into a bracelet around the hand so that both ends are accessible. The single is then plied back on itself without needing a second spindle or bobbin.

content.text: When you spin a
singles
, the twist is active and the yarn will kink.
Plying
two singles together in the opposite direction balances the stored twist and produces a round, stable yarn.
Andean plying
lets you ply a full cop without needing a second spindle: the yarn is wound into a bracelet on the hand so both ends are accessible at once.
Method
Step 1: Wind the Andean bracelet

Hold your non-dominant hand palm up. Tuck the yarn tail between your ring and little finger, leaving 20 cm free. Wind the yarn from the cop in a figure-eight pattern: bring it over your index finger, under the palm, over the little finger, under the palm, and repeat. Continue until the full cop is wound. Tuck the final end under the last wrap. You now have a bracelet with two accessible ends: the tucked tail at the bottom and the final end at the top.
Step 2: Set up for plying
Tie both yarn ends together with a loose overhand knot and attach them to the spindle hook. The two strands should run side by side from your bracelet hand to the hook.
Step 3: Ply counter-clockwise

Spin the spindle counter-clockwise (S-direction, the reverse of your original Z-spin). Hold both strands together in your top hand and allow the spindle to insert plying twist. The two singles will wind around each other. Do not let the spindle run so long that the plying twist travels up into the bracelet: keep the bracelet hand close and let yarn feed out a little at a time.
Step 4: Check the balance
Every 30 cm or so, hold the plied yarn in a loop and let the spindle hang free. A
balanced yarn
hangs without twisting. If the loop twists toward you, there is too much plying twist: spin fewer rotations per length. If the loop twists away from you, there is too little: spin more.
Step 5: Skein and set the twist

Wind the finished plied yarn onto a niddy-noddy or around your elbow and thumb to form a skein. Tie the skein in 4 places with loose figure-eight ties. Soak in warm water for 20 minutes, squeeze out excess water, snap the skein firmly 3-4 times to even the twist, and hang to dry with a small weight at the bottom.

attrs.heading: Common problems
attrs.intro: Two problems specific to spindle plying.

items.symptom: Plied yarn is over-twisted and kinky
items.cause: Too many plying rotations per length of yarn. The spindle ran too long between feeds.
items.fix: Let yarn feed out more frequently. The plied yarn should feel smooth and round as it forms, not coiled or corded.

items.symptom: Bracelet falls off the hand mid-ply
items.cause: Tension pulled the loops off the fingers during feeding.
items.fix: Keep the bracelet hand closer to the spindle so there is less tension on the loops. Feed yarn by letting the bracelet slide down rather than pulling it off.
